Re:
Fern PolrrrcAr. PnACTTcES Cor,,rMrssroN
.128 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacrarnenro. CA 95814-232t)
(916) )22'5660 o Fax (.916) 322-0886
September 2,2010
Mark Stephenson
Environmental Scientist IV
Department of Fish and Game
316 nth Sffeet
Pacific Grove, CA 95039
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-127
Dear Mr. Stephenson
This letter responds to your reqllest for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Thir letter shoulcl not be construed asissistance on any
conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(bX8XA).) In addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Cotnmission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975)
I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because
one for informal assistance.
your guestions are general in nature, we are treating your request as
Please note that our advice is based solely on the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on
the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest
or Government Code Section 1090. In addition, we offer no opinion on the post-government
employment restrictions of Public Contract Code Section 1041l. We Llrge yoll to consult your
agency's counsel or the Attomey General's office regarding these provisions.
QUESTION
Should you accept employment with the San Jose State University Research For,rndation
(the "SJSURF") upon retiring from the Califbrnia Department of Fish and Game (the "DFG"),
do the Act's revolving door prohibitions restrict you fiom working on water quality monitoring
I The Political Reform Act is contained in GovernmentCode Sections 81000 through 91014. All statubry
ret'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through lti997 of Title 2 of the California Cocle of Regulations. All
regulaklry ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 ol the Califbrnia Crlde of Regulations, unless otherwise inclicated.
: Infbrmal assistance does not provicte the requeskrr with the immunity provided by an opinion or tbrmal
written advice. (Section 83 I l4; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. I-lO-127
Page No. 2
contracts between the SJSURF and the State Water Resources Control Board (the "SWRCB,') or
between the SJSURF and the DFG.
CONCLUSION
Prior to retiring, the Act prohibits you from using your position to make, participate in
making, or influence a decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on a prospective employer. Also, should you accept employment with the SJSURF after retiring
from state service, the Act's one-year ban prohibits you lrom appearing before or communicating
with the DFG, or a state agency "which budget, personnel, ond oth". operations are subject to
the control" of the DFG, for compensation and for the purpose of influencing administrative or
legislative action, or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amend-ment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods o, prop"iy.
Additionally, the Act's permanent ban prohibits you from particlparing in a judicial oi quasi-
judicial proceeding involving the State of California or assisting ottrers in th! proceeding if the
proceeding is one in which you participated while employed bythe DFG. Note that some or all
of these provisions may not apply if the SJSURF is a state or local governmental agency.
However, because you have not provided information regarding thii issue, we are unable to
analyze whether the SJSURF qualifies as a state or local gou".n-"ntal agency.
FACTS
You are currently employed by the DFG. You plan to retire in the next few months and
are considering employment with the SJSURF. Currently, there are several ongoing contracts
for the SJSURF to conduct water quality monitoring for ihe SWRCB. Additionally, trr" onc
may also contract with the SJSURF to conduct water quality monitoring. Upon reiiring from
state service, you would like to know if the Act permits you to work on thesi contracts on the
behalf of the SJSURF.
ANALYSIS
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act, colloquially known as the'tevolving doir" prohibitions.
In addition' Section 87407 prohibits officials from making, participating inlnakirg, o. using
their position to inflttence decisions affecting persons with whom ifr"y ore negotiaiing
employment, or have any arrangement conceming employment.
l. Negotiating Prospective Employment
A public official may negotiate and accept an of-fer of future employment before leaving
his or her current state position. However, Section 87407 is designed to ensure that the official-
does not use his or her state position to make any clecisions that,rn,luty benefit the organization
that is hiring the official. Section 97407 states:


File No. I-10-127
Page No. 3
"No public official shall make, participate in making, or Llse his or her official
position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person
with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any amangement concerning, 
-
prospective employment."
A pttblic official is "negotiating" employment "when he or she interviews or discusses an
offer of employment with an employer or his or her agenr." (Regulation ftTa7@)e).) A public
official has an "arrangement" concelling prospective employ*"nt *h"n he or she accepts^an
offer of employment. (Regulat ion IB1 47 (cX2). )
If you have "negotiated" prospective employment or have made an "arrangement"
regarding prospective employment with the SJSURF as discussed above, you are prohibited
under Section 87407 from making, participating in making, or using your official position to
influence any govemmental decisions "directly relating" to the SfStiRf'.3 A govemmental
decision "directly relates" to a prospective employer if the public official knows or has reason to
know that the employer, or the employer's agent, has eithei (l) initiated the proceeding in which
the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar r"qu.rt or (2) is a
named party in, or subject of, the proceeding. (Regulations lg704.l(a) and tu+lb)0).) A
governmental decision also "directly relates" to a prospective employer if the public official
knows or has reason to know that it is reasonably foreseeable thai thi financiai effect of the
decision on a prospective employer is material. The financial effect of a decision on a
prospective employer is material if the effect meets the materiality thresholds established under
Regulation 18705.1(c) for a business entity, Regulation 18705.3(b)(2) for a nonprofit
organization, or Regulation 18705.3(bX3) for an individual. (Regulation 18747(Oe).)
lf you need additional assistance in determining whether you may participate in any
specific decision that may affect a prospective employer such ur ih. SrSUitn, it is advisabie that
you seek fttrther advice providing all relevant facts relating to the decision.
2. Post-Governmental Employment provisions
One'Year B1n' The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for
compensation, any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communiiation,
before his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative
actionsa or any discretionary act involving itre issuance, o-"ridrirt, awarding, or revocation of a
I we note that Regulation 18747(d)(3) specitically stares that the prohibitions of Section g7407 do not
apply if "[tlhe prospective employer is a state, local, or f'ederal governmentil agency." However, you have not
provided any indication as to whether the SJSURF may be a rtai. or local goveinmental agency under the Act. (For
lactors that influence the determination of whether an entity is a state or local gou..n,,.ntit aglncy see the
Commission's opinion In re Siegel (lg'17) 3 FppC Ops. 62.) We proceed with-our analysis uiO., it. assumption
that the SJSURF is not a state or local governmental agency.
. * For purposes of Section 874}6,the Act defines "administrative action" and "legislative action,'as the
fbllowing:


File No. I-rc-W
Page No.4
permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section
87 406; Regulation 187 46.L)
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-oi-interest code.
(Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(aX2).)5 The ban applies for rwelve months from the
date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment.6 While in effect, the one-year
ban applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her
appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an
agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 1g746.1(b)(3) and (a).)
In contrast to the petmanent ban, which only applies to 'Judicial or quasi-judicial"
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the
pllrpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5).) An
appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal
puryose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the
action or proceeding." (Regulation L8746.2.) An appearance or communication includes, but is
not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic
transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communicaiion. (/d.)
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (l) before a state
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state ug"n"y ,.which
budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state og"niy the public
official worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to th; direction and
control of the Govemor, if the official was a designated employee olthe Governor,s office
"'Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development; consideration,
amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, reguiation, or other action in
any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding . . .." (Section 82002(a).)
"'Legislative action' means the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification.
enactment or det'eat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the
Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof,
or by a member or. employee of the Legislature acting in his otficial capacity. 'Legislative action'
also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." (Section'SZ0:2.)
5 A governmental employee should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-ofl-interest code if the
employee makes or participates in making governmental decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable material eff'ect
on any financial interest. (Section 87302.\
For purposes of the one-year ban, the date on which an offlcial permanently leaves office or employment
is the date on lvhich the otficial is no longer authorized to perform the duties of that oifi.. o, employment, and stops
performing those duties. A person shall not be deemed to have left office permanently because he or she is on a
leave of absence or serves as an intermittent employee. However, a person shall be cleemed to have lett otflce
permanently if the person merely receives compensation tbr accrued leave credits. (Regulation lgl46.1(b))


File No. l-10-127
Page No. 5
during the twelve months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation
18746.1(bX6).)
However, not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban. Appearances or
communications before a former state agency employer, made as part of "servicei performed to
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract,
or sale agreement may be exchlded from the [one-yearl prohibitions . . . provided the services do
not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or
proceedings." (Regulation 18746.1(bXs)(A); Quiring Advice Letter, No. 4-03-272; Hcrnctn
Advice Letter, No. t-00-209.)
Additionally, Regulation 18746.2(b)(1)-(4) provides that appearances or communicarions
are not restricted under the one-year ban, if an individual:
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar
public event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject
matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) Communicates with the press."
Without violating the one-year ban, a former agency official may also draft proposals on
a client's behalf to be submitted to the official's former agency so long is the former official is
not identified in connection with the client's efforts to influence the agency. (CookAdvice
Letter, No. 4-95-321; Harrisorc Advice Letter, No. 4-92-289.) Similarly, a former agency
official may Llse his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements, plani, or
policies of the official's former agency so long as the employee is not identified with ihe
employer's efforts to influence the agency. (perry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.)7
You have not specified whether you are a designated employee of the DFG. Should you
accept employment with the SJSURF, and assuming you are either a designated employee of the
DFG or should be a designated employee of the DFG, appearances and communicaiions before
the DFG, or any other state agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to
the control" of the DFG, on behalf of the SJSURF are prohibited for o ene-year period if made
for the pu{pose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or inflgencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a peimit, li..rrr",
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase ofgoods or property.
7 Aclditionally, the one-year ban does not apply to a fbrmer state official who is or becomes an otflcer or
employee of another state agency and is making an appearance or communication on behalf of that agency. (Section
87406(eX t). Should you accept employment with the SJSURF, the one-year ban does not apply ro appearances or
communications made on the behalf of the SJSURF if the SJSURF qualii'ies as a state governmentat igency.


File No. I-10-127
Page No. 6
More specifically, you have asked whether the one-year ban prohibits you from working
on water quality monitoring contracts between the SJSURF and the SWRCB or between the
SJSURF and the DFG. As outlined above, the one-year ban prohibits you from appearing, or
making a communication, be.fore your former state employer or a state agency "*hi.h bu-dget,
personnel, and other operations are subject to the control" of your former statl agency.
Accordingly, the one-year ban does not apply to an appearance or commllnication before the
SWRCB, which is not subject to the DFG's control.
As for contracts between the SJSURF and the DFG, only appearances before or
communication with the DFG for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action,
or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awaiding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property are
prohibited. While an appearance or communication before the DFG to influence the awarding of
a contract is prohibited, you are not prohibited from assisting the SJSURF behind the scenes in
drafting a proposal, so long as you are not identified in any way when the proposal is submitted.
Furthermore, the one-year ban does not prohibit your from performing the tecirnical task of
monitoring water quality under an awarded contract because this task does not involve an
appearance or commllnication for the pllrpose of influencing administrative or legislative action,
or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awaiding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or-property.
Permanent Ban - The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from
"switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any specific procied-ing involving the
State of California or assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the
former state employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and, g74O2;
Regtrlation 18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes
a leave of absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation tgZ+t.i(axt).)8
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or
any oral or written communication--or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or is.sisting in
representing any other person, other than the State of Califomia, in an appearan"e o,
communication--made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official.e
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any .o.,r1 or rtot"
8 "The date on which an ottlcial permanently leaves otfice or employment or takes a leave 9f absence is
the date on which the otflcial is no longer authorized to pertilrm the duties of ihe offlce or employment, and the
otficial skrps perfbrming those duties, even if the official continues to receive compensation for accrued leave
credits." (Regulation I 8746.a(aX I ).)
e Pursuant to Sections 87401 and 87402,the permanent ban does not apply if the former state otl1cial is
repre-senting or assisting the State of Calitbrnia. Like the one-year ban, the perminent ban does not apply to your
activities on behalf of the SJSURF if the SJSURF qualities as a srate governmental agency


File No. I-10-127
Page No. 7
administrative agency. . .." (Section 87400(c).) Additionally, an official'is consiclered to have
"participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and
substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, renclering
advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or Llse of confidential information. . .." (Section
87400(d).)
"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated.
A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves different po.ti"r, a
different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous
proceedings." (Risf Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-
119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not
participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. I-99-104; Antlersorc Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or
approval. (Anderson, supro; Blonien Advice Letter, No. 4-89-463.)
While we have detailed the general provisions of the permanent ban for your review, you
have not provided any information related to your participation in any particular proceeding
while employed with the DFG that may affect your ability to engage in any of the conduct listed
herein. To apply the permanent ban to your situation, you need to determine if any of the actions
in which you may engage on behalf of the SJSURF involve a proceeding in whictr you
previously participated. (Regul ati on 187 4 l .l (aX4). )
For example, if you were involved in drafting or negotiating any particular contract to
conduct water quality monitoring as an employee of the DFG, you have previously participated
in a'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" and are perrnanently baned from ,:switching
sides" and participating in the same proceeding on behalf of the SJSURF. If you need additional
assistance relating to a specific proceeding in which you previously participaied, you should seek
further advice providing all relevant facts.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act, colloquially known as the'tevolving doir" prohibitions.
In addition' Section 87407 prohibits officials from making, participating inlnakirg, o. using
their position to inflttence decisions affecting persons with whom ifr"y ore negotiaiing
employment, or have any arrangement conceming employment.
l. Negotiating Prospective Employment
A public official may negotiate and accept an of-fer of future employment before leaving
his or her current state position. However, Section 87407 is designed to ensure that the official-
does not use his or her state position to make any clecisions that,rn,luty benefit the organization
that is hiring the official. Section 97407 states:
"No public official shall make, participate in making, or Llse his or her official
position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person
with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any amangement concerning, 
-
prospective employment."
A pttblic official is "negotiating" employment "when he or she interviews or discusses an
offer of employment with an employer or his or her agenr." (Regulation ftTa7@)e).) A public
official has an "arrangement" concelling prospective employ*"nt *h"n he or she accepts^an
offer of employment. (Regulat ion IB1 47 (cX2). )
If you have "negotiated" prospective employment or have made an "arrangement"
regarding prospective employment with the SJSURF as discussed above, you are prohibited
under Section 87407 from making, participating in making, or using your official position to
influence any govemmental decisions "directly relating" to the SfStiRf'.3 A govemmental
decision "directly relates" to a prospective employer if the public official knows or has reason to
know that the employer, or the employer's agent, has eithei (l) initiated the proceeding in which
the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar r"qu.rt or (2) is a
named party in, or subject of, the proceeding. (Regulations lg704.l(a) and tu+lb)0).) A
governmental decision also "directly relates" to a prospective employer if the public official
knows or has reason to know that it is reasonably foreseeable thai thi financiai effect of the
decision on a prospective employer is material. The financial effect of a decision on a
prospective employer is material if the effect meets the materiality thresholds established under
Regulation 18705.1(c) for a business entity, Regulation 18705.3(b)(2) for a nonprofit
organization, or Regulation 18705.3(bX3) for an individual. (Regulation 18747(Oe).)
lf you need additional assistance in determining whether you may participate in any
specific decision that may affect a prospective employer such ur ih. SrSUitn, it is advisabie that
you seek fttrther advice providing all relevant facts relating to the decision.
2. Post-Governmental Employment provisions
One'Year B1n' The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for
compensation, any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communiiation,
before his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative
actionsa or any discretionary act involving itre issuance, o-"ridrirt, awarding, or revocation of a
I we note that Regulation 18747(d)(3) specitically stares that the prohibitions of Section g7407 do not
apply if "[tlhe prospective employer is a state, local, or f'ederal governmentil agency." However, you have not
provided any indication as to whether the SJSURF may be a rtai. or local goveinmental agency under the Act. (For
lactors that influence the determination of whether an entity is a state or local gou..n,,.ntit aglncy see the
Commission's opinion In re Siegel (lg'17) 3 FppC Ops. 62.) We proceed with-our analysis uiO., it. assumption
that the SJSURF is not a state or local governmental agency.
. * For purposes of Section 874}6,the Act defines "administrative action" and "legislative action,'as the
fbllowing:

permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section
87 406; Regulation 187 46.L)
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-oi-interest code.
(Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(aX2).)5 The ban applies for rwelve months from the
date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment.6 While in effect, the one-year
ban applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her
appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an
agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 1g746.1(b)(3) and (a).)
In contrast to the petmanent ban, which only applies to 'Judicial or quasi-judicial"
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the
pllrpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5).) An
appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal
puryose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the
action or proceeding." (Regulation L8746.2.) An appearance or communication includes, but is
not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic
transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communicaiion. (/d.)
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (l) before a state

official worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to th; direction and
control of the Govemor, if the official was a designated employee olthe Governor,s office
"'Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development; consideration,
amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, reguiation, or other action in
any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding . . .." (Section 82002(a).)
"'Legislative action' means the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification.
enactment or det'eat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the
Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof,
or by a member or. employee of the Legislature acting in his otficial capacity. 'Legislative action'
also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." (Section'SZ0:2.)

during the twelve months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation
18746.1(bX6).)
However, not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban. Appearances or
communications before a former state agency employer, made as part of "servicei performed to
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract,
or sale agreement may be exchlded from the [one-yearl prohibitions . . . provided the services do
not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or
proceedings." (Regulation 18746.1(bXs)(A); Quiring Advice Letter, No. 4-03-272; Hcrnctn
Advice Letter, No. t-00-209.)
Additionally, Regulation 18746.2(b)(1)-(4) provides that appearances or communicarions
are not restricted under the one-year ban, if an individual:
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar
public event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject
matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) Communicates with the press."
Without violating the one-year ban, a former agency official may also draft proposals on
a client's behalf to be submitted to the official's former agency so long is the former official is
not identified in connection with the client's efforts to influence the agency. (CookAdvice
Letter, No. 4-95-321; Harrisorc Advice Letter, No. 4-92-289.) Similarly, a former agency
official may Llse his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements, plani, or
policies of the official's former agency so long as the employee is not identified with ihe
employer's efforts to influence the agency. (perry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.)7
You have not specified whether you are a designated employee of the DFG. Should you
accept employment with the SJSURF, and assuming you are either a designated employee of the
DFG or should be a designated employee of the DFG, appearances and communicaiions before
the DFG, or any other state agency "which budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to
the control" of the DFG, on behalf of the SJSURF are prohibited for o ene-year period if made
for the pu{pose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or inflgencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a peimit, li..rrr",
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase ofgoods or property.
7 Aclditionally, the one-year ban does not apply to a fbrmer state official who is or becomes an otflcer or
employee of another state agency and is making an appearance or communication on behalf of that agency. (Section
87406(eX t). Should you accept employment with the SJSURF, the one-year ban does not apply ro appearances or
communications made on the behalf of the SJSURF if the SJSURF qualii'ies as a state governmentat igency.
More specifically, you have asked whether the one-year ban prohibits you from working
on water quality monitoring contracts between the SJSURF and the SWRCB or between the
SJSURF and the DFG. As outlined above, the one-year ban prohibits you from appearing, or
making a communication, be.fore your former state employer or a state agency "*hi.h bu-dget,
personnel, and other operations are subject to the control" of your former statl agency.
Accordingly, the one-year ban does not apply to an appearance or commllnication before the
SWRCB, which is not subject to the DFG's control.
As for contracts between the SJSURF and the DFG, only appearances before or
communication with the DFG for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action,
or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awaiding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property are
prohibited. While an appearance or communication before the DFG to influence the awarding of
a contract is prohibited, you are not prohibited from assisting the SJSURF behind the scenes in
drafting a proposal, so long as you are not identified in any way when the proposal is submitted.
Furthermore, the one-year ban does not prohibit your from performing the tecirnical task of
monitoring water quality under an awarded contract because this task does not involve an
appearance or commllnication for the pllrpose of influencing administrative or legislative action,
or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awaiding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or-property.
Permanent Ban - The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from
"switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any specific procied-ing involving the
State of California or assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the
former state employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and, g74O2;
Regtrlation 18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes
a leave of absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation tgZ+t.i(axt).)8
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or
any oral or written communication--or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or is.sisting in
representing any other person, other than the State of Califomia, in an appearan"e o,
communication--made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official.e
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any .o.,r1 or rtot"
8 "The date on which an ottlcial permanently leaves otfice or employment or takes a leave 9f absence is
the date on which the otflcial is no longer authorized to pertilrm the duties of ihe offlce or employment, and the
otficial skrps perfbrming those duties, even if the official continues to receive compensation for accrued leave
credits." (Regulation I 8746.a(aX I ).)
e Pursuant to Sections 87401 and 87402,the permanent ban does not apply if the former state otl1cial is
repre-senting or assisting the State of Calitbrnia. Like the one-year ban, the perminent ban does not apply to your
activities on behalf of the SJSURF if the SJSURF qualities as a srate governmental agency
administrative agency. . .." (Section 87400(c).) Additionally, an official'is consiclered to have
"participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and
substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, renclering
advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or Llse of confidential information. . .." (Section
87400(d).)
"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated.
A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves different po.ti"r, a
different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous
proceedings." (Risf Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-
119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not
participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. I-99-104; Antlersorc Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or
approval. (Anderson, supro; Blonien Advice Letter, No. 4-89-463.)
While we have detailed the general provisions of the permanent ban for your review, you
have not provided any information related to your participation in any particular proceeding
while employed with the DFG that may affect your ability to engage in any of the conduct listed
herein. To apply the permanent ban to your situation, you need to determine if any of the actions
in which you may engage on behalf of the SJSURF involve a proceeding in whictr you
previously participated. (Regul ati on 187 4 l .l (aX4). )
For example, if you were involved in drafting or negotiating any particular contract to
conduct water quality monitoring as an employee of the DFG, you have previously participated
in a'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" and are perrnanently baned from ,:switching
sides" and participating in the same proceeding on behalf of the SJSURF. If you need additional
assistance relating to a specific proceeding in which you previously participaied, you should seek
further advice providing all relevant facts.

Additionally, the Act's permanent ban prohibits you from particlparing in a judicial oi quasi-

of these provisions may not apply if the SJSURF is a state or local governmental agency.
However, because you have not provided information regarding thii issue, we are unable to
